heroOverview: Pseudopelade of Brocq is a rare primary lymphocytic scarring alopecia that destroys hair follicles and replaces them with fibrotic tissue, producing smooth, skin-coloured patches of permanent hair loss that merge over time into irregular 'footprints in the snow' on the scalp. It was first described by the French dermatologist Louis-Anne-Jean Brocq in 1885 and remains one of the least understood primary cicatricial alopecias. Incidence estimates range from 3 to 6 cases per 100,000 people per year, with women affected approximately twice as often as men. Pseudopelade of Brocq is notable for its insidious onset and minimal inflammation: unlike the fiery red plaques of lichen planopilaris or the scaling of discoid lupus, the patches are pale, slightly depressed, and almost asymptomatic. Hair loss is permanent once fibrosis is established, making early specialist diagnosis and immunosuppressive treatment critical to limit the extent of destruction.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated incidence of approximately 3-6 per 100,000 people per year; rare overall but one of the more commonly diagnosed primary cicatricial alopecias
keyStats.demographics: Women approximately twice as often as men; most common presentation in adults aged 30-60
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of presentation approximately 40-50 years; rare in children
keyStats.globalCases: Precise global figures are unavailable; pseudopelade of Brocq is diagnosed worldwide in all ethnicities without clear geographic clustering

definition: Pseudopelade of Brocq (ICD-10: L66.0) is a primary lymphocytic cicatricial (scarring) alopecia in which a perifollicular T-lymphocyte infiltrate selectively destroys the stem cells of the follicular bulge and isthmus, leading to permanent fibrotic replacement of the follicular unit. The defining histological and clinical feature is the near-complete absence of epidermal or surface inflammation: there is no scaling, erythema, or follicular plugging as seen in lichen planopilaris, and no atrophy or dyspigmentation as in discoid lupus erythematosus. Instead, the scalp surface within the patch appears smooth, faintly depressed (atrophic), and skin-coloured, with one or two residual hairs sometimes persisting at the patch centre. The disease progresses slowly over years, with new satellite patches appearing at the margins and coalescing into larger irregular bald areas. Pseudopelade of Brocq falls under dermatology; its diagnosis requires histopathological confirmation because the clinical features overlap with burnt-out lichen planopilaris and end-stage discoid lupus erythematosus. The condition is named 'pseudopelade' because its appearance mimics pelade (the French term for alopecia areata) without having its autoimmune mechanism or potential for spontaneous recovery.

typesClassification.description: Discrete, smooth, skin-coloured atrophic patches of 0.5-2 cm diameter on the vertex and parietal scalp, coalescing slowly into irregular plaques. Minimal inflammation detectable clinically or histologically.

Dermoscopy reveals perifollicular erythema and subtle scale at the margins of expanding patches; this phase is histologically characterised by a moderate lymphocytic infiltrate at the infundibulum and isthmus.

Fibrosis is established, inflammation has resolved histologically, and the patches are stable in size. Clinically indistinguishable from end-stage lichen planopilaris or late discoid lupus erythematosus.

primarySymptoms: Smooth, slightly depressed (atrophic), skin-coloured patches of permanent hair loss on the vertex or parietal scalp, typically 0.5-2 cm in diameter.
Gradual, asymptomatic enlargement and coalescence of patches over months to years, producing the characteristic irregular 'footprints in the snow' pattern.
Near-complete absence of visible scalp inflammation within the patches: no redness, scaling, or follicular plugging that characterises other scarring alopecias.
Loss of follicular orifices within affected areas when examined under dermoscopy — a direct sign that follicles have been replaced by fibrous tissue.
One or a few solitary residual hairs occasionally persisting within the centre of an established bald patch.
Mild pruritus or a subtle burning sensation reported by approximately 30-40% of patients, especially during active phases of disease extension.
Progressive widening of the total bald area as new satellite patches appear at the periphery of existing plaques.
Faint whitish or ivory discolouration of the scalp in large coalesced patches compared with the surrounding unaffected scalp.
No evidence of hair regrowth within established patches — a key distinguishing feature from alopecia areata, in which regrowth is common.
Occasionally, a few hairs with peripilar white scale visible at the very edge of the active margin under magnification, indicating a zone of active follicle destruction.
earlyWarningSigns: A small area of scalp on the vertex where hair seems permanently reduced compared with surrounding regions, without any obvious prior trauma or illness.
Subtle depression of the scalp surface in a small area even before hair loss becomes visually prominent.
Slight burning or itching on a specific area of the scalp that cannot be explained by dandruff or contact irritation.
Two or three adjacent small bald spots that are unusually smooth and pale, unlike the normal scalp texture.
emergencySigns: Rapid expansion of the bald area over weeks rather than months, which suggests a more inflammatory co-existing condition or misdiagnosis.
New follicular pustules, thick scaling, or marked erythema developing within or at the margin of existing patches, requiring urgent biopsy to exclude folliculitis decalvans or acne keloidalis nuchae.
Severe psychological distress, suicidal ideation, or acute depressive episode precipitated by progressive hair loss, requiring immediate mental health assessment.

causes.description: CD4+ T-lymphocytes and CD8+ T-lymphocytes accumulate preferentially around the infundibulum and isthmus of the follicle, inducing apoptosis and fibrotic replacement of the bulge stem cell niche. Without stem cell replenishment, the follicle cannot cycle and is permanently lost.

Healthy hair follicles are protected from immune attack by local immune-suppressive signals including MHC class I downregulation and regulatory T-cell activity. In primary cicatricial alopecias, these protective mechanisms fail, exposing the follicle to autoimmune destruction. The precise trigger for privilege collapse in pseudopelade of Brocq is not fully established.

There is an ongoing histopathological debate about whether classic pseudopelade of Brocq is an entity distinct from end-stage lichen planopilaris or represents the same disease at a quiescent phase. Some investigators propose that most cases classified as pseudopelade of Brocq represent inactive LPP in which early inflammatory features have been missed. Active pseudopelade does show a distinct histological profile with fewer interface changes than LPP, supporting some degree of biological difference.

Case series and retrospective studies have proposed a role for Borrelia burgdorferi spirochaetal infection in initiating pseudopelade in endemic regions of Europe, though North American and other studies have not confirmed this association, and the evidence remains inconclusive. A triggering inflammatory insult to the follicle in genetically predisposed individuals remains a working hypothesis.

Pseudopelade of Brocq occurs more frequently in individuals with a personal or family history of other autoimmune conditions, suggesting a shared genetic susceptibility to autoimmune follicular disease, though specific HLA associations have not been consistently identified in this rare disorder.

riskFactors.description: Women are affected approximately twice as often as men, a sex ratio common to many primary lymphocytic cicatricial alopecias. Hormonal and immunological differences between sexes are thought to contribute.
The condition is most frequently diagnosed in mid-adult life. Diagnosis in children under age 16 is unusual and warrants consideration of other scarring alopecias.

riskFactors.factor: Personal history of lichen planus or lichen planopilaris
riskFactors.description: Given the histological overlap between pseudopelade of Brocq and burnt-out lichen planopilaris, patients with active LPP have an implicit risk of late-stage confluent scarring that clinically resembles pseudopelade.

Thyroid autoimmune disease, discoid lupus erythematosus, and morphoea are reported more frequently in patient cohorts with primary cicatricial alopecias than in the general population, suggesting shared immune dysregulation.

The lack of visible scalp inflammation in pseudopelade means patients and even clinicians may dismiss early patches as insignificant. Every month of delay allows further follicular destruction. Early specialist referral and prompt biopsy limit the extent of scarring.

In European endemic regions for Lyme disease, a subset of primary cicatricial alopecia patients show serological evidence of B. burgdorferi infection. Whether this represents a trigger or co-incidence remains under investigation.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 30-60, who account for approximately two-thirds of all diagnosed cases
Adults with a history of lichen planopilaris or relapsing-remitting scalp inflammation
Patients with concurrent autoimmune conditions including discoid lupus and thyroid disease
Individuals of all ethnicities; no strong racial predilection has been consistently documented
Rarely, children and adolescents in whom the diagnosis requires careful histological confirmation to exclude other cicatricial alopecias
diagnosisOverview:
Pseudopelade of Brocq cannot be diagnosed on clinical appearance alone and requires a 4 mm punch biopsy from the active margin for histopathological confirmation. Clinically, the patches are smooth, pale, and atrophic with follicular dropout visible on dermoscopy; the absence of follicular ostia within patches is a key dermoscopic sign. Active margins may show subtle perifollicular erythema on dermoscopy even when invisible to the naked eye. The histological hallmark is a perifollicular lymphocytic infiltrate at the level of the isthmus and infundibulum, without epidermal involvement and without the interface change (civatte body formation) that characterises lichen planopilaris. Late fibrotic stage shows only follicular dropout and fibrous tracts without residual inflammation. Direct immunofluorescence (DIF) of the biopsy sample is performed to exclude discoid lupus erythematosus, which shows granular immunoglobulin and complement deposition at the dermal-epidermal junction; pseudopelade of Brocq shows absent or non-specific DIF.

Blood tests including antinuclear antibody (ANA), anti-dsDNA, complement C3 and C4, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are ordered to exclude systemic lupus erythematosus. Thyroid function and anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies screen for associated autoimmune thyroid disease. Trichoscopy (dermoscopy) is an invaluable non-invasive tool for monitoring activity: loss of follicular ostia, fibrotic white patches, and perifollicular scale at the margin are monitored sequentially to gauge disease activity and treatment response.

diagnosticTests.test: 4 mm punch biopsy with haematoxylin-eosin staining
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms follicular fibrosis and the lymphocytic infiltrate pattern; distinguishes pseudopelade of Brocq from lichen planopilaris, discoid lupus erythematosus, and alopecia areata.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Under local anaesthetic, a small circular skin core (4 mm) is taken from the active margin of the alopecia patch; the sample is processed and reported by a dermatopathologist within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes discoid lupus erythematosus by looking for granular immunoglobulin (IgG, IgM) and complement deposition along the basement membrane, which is characteristic of DLE but absent in pseudopelade.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A second biopsy sample from the same site is processed in a specialist immunofluorescence laboratory; results take 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies loss of follicular ostia within the patch, perifollicular fibrotic white areas, and subtle peripilar scale at the active margin. Used to map disease activity and monitor response to treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The dermatologist applies a polarised light dermoscope to multiple sites on the scalp; painless examination takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for systemic lupus erythematosus, which can co-exist with or mimic cicatricial alopecia and requires systemic treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; ANA testing is reported as positive or negative with titre; further specific antibody testing is performed for positive results.

diagnosticTests.test: Thyroid function and anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Hashimoto's thyroiditis or Graves' disease, which occur at higher frequency in autoimmune scalp disorders.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Serology for Borrelia burgdorferi (in endemic regions)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Investigates a possible infectious trigger in patients from Lyme-endemic areas in Europe and parts of North America where the association has been proposed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for ELISA and confirmatory Western blot if positive; results in 2-5 days.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment of pseudopelade of Brocq has two goals: halting the inflammatory destruction of remaining follicles and preventing further extension. Established scarring within existing patches is permanent — no treatment restores destroyed follicles — so the urgency of intervention scales directly with the degree of active disease.

First-line treatment consists of high-potency topical corticosteroids (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) applied nightly to the scalp, or intralesional triamcinolone acetonide injections (5-10 mg/mL) every 4-6 weeks into active margins. These reduce the perifollicular lymphocytic infiltrate and slow disease progression. Hydroxychloroquine 200-400 mg daily (an antimalarial with immunomodulatory properties) is the most widely used oral agent for pseudopelade of Brocq and other primary lymphocytic cicatricial alopecias; response is gradual over 3-6 months and requires G6PD screening and annual ophthalmological monitoring for retinal toxicity.

For patients who fail hydroxychloroquine, second-line agents used in specialist practice include oral mycophenolate mofetil (1-1.5 g twice daily), cyclosporine, or combination regimens. Tetracycline antibiotics (doxycycline 100 mg twice daily) have both anti-inflammatory and possible anti-spirochaetal properties and are sometimes used when Borrelia sensitisation is suspected. Treatment of confirmed Borrelia infection with standard antibiotic regimens is undertaken, though its effect on the alopecia itself is unpredictable.

Disease monitoring uses sequential dermoscopy and patient-reported symptom tracking rather than standardised validated scoring systems, as none have been universally adopted for pseudopelade. Once disease has been stable (no new patches, no active inflammation on dermoscopy) for 12-24 months, treatment may be cautiously de-escalated.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide injections
medicalTreatments.description: Triamcinolone 5-10 mg/mL injected directly into the active margins of alopecia patches every 4-6 weeks. Delivers high-concentration corticosteroid to the perifollicular infiltrate with minimal systemic exposure. First-line treatment for limited active disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilisation of disease progression in approximately 50-70% of patients with limited active pseudopelade of Brocq in published case series; does not restore already-scarred follicles.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical high-potency corticosteroids
medicalTreatments.description: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% solution or foam applied nightly to active areas or under occlusion. Used as monotherapy for mild disease or as an adjunct to systemic treatment. Periungual skin and hairline margins are the primary target zones.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest stabilisation in approximately 30-40% of patients as monotherapy; most effective when used early in disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine
medicalTreatments.description: An antimalarial immunomodulator taken orally at 200-400 mg daily (dosed at 5 mg/kg lean body weight to reduce retinal toxicity risk). The most widely used systemic agent for primary lymphocytic cicatricial alopecias. Requires G6PD testing before initiation and annual ophthalmological review.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Disease stabilisation — no new bald patches — in approximately 50-60% of patients at 12 months of treatment in the largest retrospective series; complete cessation of activity in a smaller proportion.

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil
medicalTreatments.description: An oral immunosuppressant (1-1.5 g twice daily) that inhibits T and B lymphocyte proliferation, used for pseudopelade refractory to hydroxychloroquine or intralesional corticosteroids. Requires baseline blood count and liver function monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilisation of scarring alopecia progression in approximately 50-70% of refractory cases in published cohorts from specialist centres; maintenance therapy is required.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline
medicalTreatments.description: A tetracycline antibiotic (100 mg twice daily) with well-documented anti-inflammatory properties targeting MMP inhibition and lymphocyte activation. Used both for suspected Borrelia-associated cases and as an anti-inflammatory agent in patients unable to take hydroxychloroquine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Anti-inflammatory stabilisation of cicatricial alopecia activity in approximately 30-50% of patients in small case series; combination with hydroxychloroquine is used in specialist practice.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclosporine
medicalTreatments.description: A calcineurin inhibitor that suppresses T-lymphocyte activation, used for acute or rapidly progressive pseudopelade of Brocq in short courses (3-6 months, 3-5 mg/kg/day) while longer-acting agents are initiated. Blood pressure and renal function monitoring are mandatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid reduction in active inflammation in approximately 60-70% of patients used in acute phases; not suitable for long-term maintenance due to cumulative nephrotoxicity.

surgicalOptions.name: Hair transplantation (follicular unit excision or strip graft) into stable scars
surgicalOptions.description: In carefully selected patients whose disease has been clinically and dermoscopically stable for at least 2 years, hair transplantation into scarred areas using follicular unit grafts from unaffected donor areas can partially restore cosmetic coverage. Stable scalp environment is an absolute prerequisite; active disease contraindicates transplant.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft survival rates of 50-80% have been reported in stable cicatricial alopecia when performed by experienced hair restoration surgeons; lower than in non-scarring alopecia due to fibrotic scalp vascularity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Scalp camouflage (fibres, pigment sprays, scalp micropigmentation)
alternativeTreatments.description: Keratin fibres and scalp micropigmentation (medical tattooing) significantly reduce the visible contrast between scarred and unaffected scalp. Scalp micropigmentation is a permanent technique that simulates follicular density and is popular among patients with stable extensive pseudopelade who are not candidates for transplant.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hair prostheses and wigs
alternativeTreatments.description: Custom-fitted wigs and hair integration systems are medically recognised solutions for extensive scalp involvement. Some healthcare systems provide wigs on prescription for cicatricial alopecias. Vacuum-bonded systems secure the prosthesis without adhesives.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological support and CBT
alternativeTreatments.description: Cognitive-behavioural therapy and support groups specifically for scarring alopecia (such as Cicatricial Alopecia Research Foundation resources) reduce distress, improve coping, and address the social isolation that commonly accompanies progressive permanent hair loss.

whySeeSpecialist: Pseudopelade of Brocq must be managed by a dermatologist with expertise in hair loss because the diagnosis requires biopsy and pathological interpretation, the differential includes life-altering diagnoses such as systemic lupus erythematosus, and the treatment with hydroxychloroquine, mycophenolate mofetil, or cyclosporine requires specialist monitoring. Given that each untreated month can result in permanent irreversible follicular loss, prompt referral is essential whenever smooth atrophic scalp patches are identified.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a dermatologist with a specialist hair loss clinic who performs scalp biopsies with direct immunofluorescence processing, and who uses trichoscopy for sequential disease monitoring. Familiarity with hydroxychloroquine prescribing and ophthalmological co-management is important. For patients considering hair transplantation into stable scars, a hair restoration surgeon experienced in cicatricial scalp cases is essential. The Cicatricial Alopecia Research Foundation (CARF) maintains a directory of specialist clinicians.
preventionStrategies: There is no established primary prevention for pseudopelade of Brocq.
Prompt referral to a dermatologist when smooth, atrophic bald patches are noticed limits the extent of scarring by enabling earlier treatment.
Avoiding harsh chemical scalp treatments and heat styling that could damage follicles and potentially trigger an inflammatory response in susceptible individuals.
In Borrelia-endemic regions, tick-bite prevention (protective clothing, tick checks, prompt tick removal) may reduce an environmental trigger.
Regular follow-up and dermoscopy monitoring when disease is in apparent remission to detect subtle reactivation before new patches form.
lifestyleModifications: Protect the scalp from sun exposure with hats or SPF scalp spray, as UV light can exacerbate scalp inflammation and potentially worsen disease activity.
Use gentle, fragrance-free shampoos and avoid mechanical trauma to the scalp such as vigorous brushing over active areas.
Apply prescribed topical treatments consistently and at the correct times — evening application of clobetasol propionate under occlusion is standard.
Keep follow-up dermoscopy appointments every 3-6 months during active disease and every 6-12 months during remission.
Document scalp changes with timed photographs (monthly) in standard lighting to help the dermatologist track activity at each consultation.

recommended: Adequate protein, iron, and zinc to support overall hair follicle health in unaffected regions
Vitamin D and omega-3 sources (oily fish, flaxseed) given their roles in immune regulation, though specific evidence in pseudopelade is limited
Balanced diet to support immune health during hydroxychloroquine or immunosuppressive therapy
avoid: Excess alcohol, which may interact with mycophenolate mofetil and impair immune monitoring
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice during cyclosporine therapy, as it inhibits CYP3A4 and can raise cyclosporine levels to toxic concentrations
Crash dieting that could trigger telogen effluvium superimposed on existing scarring hair loss

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise supports general wellbeing, reduces stress, and may help manage the anxiety and depression that accompany progressive hair loss. There are no disease-specific exercise restrictions for pseudopelade of Brocq. Patients on cyclosporine should monitor blood pressure during exercise. Headwear is recommended to protect the scarred scalp from sun and mechanical trauma during outdoor activities.
dailyManagement: Apply topical clobetasol as prescribed to the active margins nightly; avoid the forehead and face.
Take hydroxychloroquine consistently with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects — consistent daily timing optimises absorption.
Keep a dermatology appointment diary and never defer reviews, as disease can silently reactivate.
Report new areas of hair loss or new scalp symptoms (burning, itching) promptly rather than waiting for the next scheduled appointment.
Engage with psychosocial support: peer support from CARF or equivalent organisations provides significant emotional benefit when managing a chronic, progressively scarring condition.
prognosis: Pseudopelade of Brocq is a chronic, slowly progressive disease in which each cycle of activity produces permanent scarring. Established bald patches do not recover hair growth once fibrosis is complete. The disease course is highly variable: some patients experience decades of slow, intermittent progression before the disease burns out spontaneously, while others show more rapid expansion over a few years. The natural burn-out rate — disease entering stable quiescence without treatment — is observed in a subset of patients, though the proportion is not well defined because most are treated. With hydroxychloroquine treatment, disease stabilisation is achieved in approximately 50-60% of patients over 12 months. The key prognostic factor is the proportion of the scalp already affected at the time of diagnosis: patients presenting with small patches confined to a limited area can preserve the majority of their scalp with timely intervention. Patients with extensive involvement at presentation face a more limited cosmetic prognosis. Psychological morbidity is significant and correlates with extent of involvement, age of onset, and visibility of the alopecia.
recoveryTimeline: There is no recovery of previously scarred areas. In patients who achieve disease stabilisation with treatment, no new bald patches appear and the existing patches become stable within 3-6 months of effective therapy. Confirming remission requires stable dermoscopy findings for at least 12-24 months. Attempts at treatment de-escalation should be undertaken cautiously with continued dermoscopy monitoring every 6 months, as relapse after stopping hydroxychloroquine is reported in approximately 30-50% of cases. Hair transplantation into stable areas, where appropriate, provides cosmetic restoration 12-18 months after grafting.
complications: Permanent progressive hair loss culminating in extensive scalp alopecia if disease is not treated or does not respond to treatment.
Significant psychological morbidity — depression, anxiety, and reduced quality of life — occurring in a substantial proportion of patients with progressively visible permanent hair loss.
Retinal toxicity from long-term hydroxychloroquine use, a rare but serious complication requiring annual ophthalmological screening after 5 years of treatment.
Immunosuppression-related infections during mycophenolate mofetil or cyclosporine therapy, including reactivation of latent infections.
Delayed diagnosis and misclassification as alopecia areata leading to prolonged ineffective treatment while scarring progresses — underscoring the importance of early biopsy.

supportResources.name: Cicatricial Alopecia Research Foundation (CARF)
supportResources.description: International patient and research organisation for cicatricial alopecias, providing physician directories, patient support networks, and research updates.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Scarring Alopecia Information
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on scarring alopecias from the UK professional dermatology body.

supportResources.name: American Hair Loss Association — Cicatricial Alopecia
supportResources.description: Overview of cicatricial alopecias including pseudopelade from a US patient education resource.

sources.title: Harries MJ et al. Lichen planopilaris. Clin Exp Dermatol. 2010;35(4):354-360

Olsen EA et al. Summary of North American Hair Research Society (NAHRS)-sponsored Workshop on Cicatricial Alopecia, Duke University Medical Center, February 10 and 11, 2001. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2003;48(1):103-110
Chiang C et al. Clinical laboratory findings and treatment responses in pseudopelade of Brocq. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2010;63(6):967-974
Iorizzo M et al. Clinical signs in the differential diagnosis of primary cicatricial alopecias. Skin Appendage Disord. 2016;1(4):172-175
Miteva M, Tosti A. Dermoscopy of alopecia and hair shaft disorders. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2012;67(3):e109-117
Vano-Galvan S et al. Primary cicatricial alopecias: review of experience. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2020;82(6):1413-1420
British Association of Dermatologists — Guidelines for the Management of Lichen Planopilaris (as a cicatricial alopecia reference guideline)

confusedWithConditions.name: Alopecia areata
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alopecia areata produces smooth bald patches that can resemble pseudopelade but its follicular ostia are preserved on dermoscopy, regrowth of fine vellus hairs is common, and histology shows the 'swarm of bees' perifollicular infiltrate without fibrosis. Alopecia areata can fully regrow; pseudopelade cannot.

confusedWithConditions.name: Discoid lupus erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: DLE produces scarring alopecia with follicular plugging, red atrophic plaques, and dyspigmentation, distinguished from pseudopelade by positive direct immunofluorescence showing IgG and complement at the basement membrane and by systemic lupus workup.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ophiasis (alopecia areata band-pattern)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ophiasis is a non-scarring alopecia: follicular ostia are preserved, dermoscopy shows alopecia areata-specific signs, and regrowth is possible. Pseudopelade shows follicular dropout and fibrosis on both dermoscopy and histology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lichen planopilaris / lichen planus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lichen planopilaris presents with follicular hyperkeratosis, perifollicular erythema, and significant pruritus alongside scarring. Interface change and civatte bodies are present histologically. Classic pseudopelade of Brocq has minimal surface inflammation and a less prominent interface reaction on biopsy.

relatedConditions.name: Discoid lupus erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: One of the most important differentials for pseudopelade; DLE produces scarring alopecia that can be clinically identical and is excluded by biopsy and immunofluorescence.

relatedConditions.name: Alopecia areata
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common non-scarring alopecia, which can mimic pseudopelade's smooth patches. The key distinction is preserved follicular ostia and potential regrowth in alopecia areata.

relatedConditions.name: Ophiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A severe subtype of alopecia areata at the opposite end of the permanence spectrum from pseudopelade; understanding the distinction between scarring and non-scarring alopecia is central to management.

relatedConditions.name: Alopecia mucinosa
relatedConditions.relevance: A rare inflammatory follicular disorder that can produce scarring alopecia and is distinguished histologically; occasionally associated with lymphoma, making accurate diagnosis critical.

faqs.question: What is pseudopelade of Brocq?
faqs.answer: Pseudopelade of Brocq is a rare type of permanent (scarring) hair loss in which the immune system destroys hair follicles and replaces them with fibrous tissue. It creates smooth, skin-coloured bald patches on the scalp that gradually enlarge and merge, resembling 'footprints in the snow'. Hair does not regrow in affected areas.

faqs.question: Is pseudopelade the same as alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: No. Alopecia areata and pseudopelade both create smooth bald patches, but alopecia areata is non-scarring and can fully regrow, while pseudopelade destroys follicles permanently. A scalp biopsy readily distinguishes them. The name 'pseudopelade' means 'false pelade' because it mimics alopecia areata's appearance without sharing its reversibility.

faqs.question: Is pseudopelade of Brocq curable?
faqs.answer: The scarred areas cannot be restored. Treatment aims to stop further follicular destruction. With hydroxychloroquine and corticosteroids, disease stabilisation is achieved in about 50-60% of patients. Once fully stable for 2 or more years, selected patients may consider hair transplantation into scarred areas for cosmetic restoration.

faqs.question: How is pseudopelade of Brocq diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a scalp punch biopsy, typically 4 mm, from the active edge of the alopecia patch. The histologist looks for perifollicular lymphocytes, fibrosis, and follicular dropout. Direct immunofluorescence is performed to exclude discoid lupus. Blood tests screen for lupus and thyroid disease.

faqs.question: What causes pseudopelade of Brocq?
faqs.answer: The underlying cause is an autoimmune lymphocytic attack that destroys the stem cell region of the hair follicle, leading to permanent fibrosis. The precise trigger is unknown. Links to lichen planopilaris, autoimmune disease, and in some European series to Borrelia (Lyme disease) infection have been proposed but not definitively established.

faqs.question: Will the bald patches from pseudopelade grow back?
faqs.answer: No. Once follicles are replaced by fibrous tissue, hair cannot regrow in that area. Treatment prevents further loss by stopping the immune attack on healthy follicles. Very early patches where inflammation has not yet completed destruction may show some stabilisation with treatment, but fully established scars are permanent.

faqs.question: How is pseudopelade treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment includes intralesional corticosteroid injections into active patch margins and hydroxychloroquine taken orally. Refractory cases are treated with mycophenolate mofetil, cyclosporine, or doxycycline. Topical high-potency corticosteroids are used as an adjunct. All treatment aims to halt progression, not reverse scarring.

faqs.question: Does pseudopelade spread to the whole scalp?
faqs.answer: Pseudopelade progresses slowly in most patients, adding new patches over months to years. Some patients experience burn-out with no further extension. A minority develop extensive scalp involvement. Early treatment significantly limits the ultimate area affected.

faqs.question: Is pseudopelade of Brocq painful or itchy?
faqs.answer: Most patients describe the patches themselves as asymptomatic. Approximately 30-40% report mild scalp burning, itching, or tenderness, usually at the active advancing margin rather than within established scars. This is generally mild compared with the pruritus of lichen planopilaris.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pseudopelade and discoid lupus?
faqs.answer: Discoid lupus causes scarring alopecia with follicular plugging, red plaques, and skin discolouration, and shows positive direct immunofluorescence on biopsy. Pseudopelade of Brocq shows smooth pale patches with no surface inflammation and negative or non-specific immunofluorescence. Both require biopsy to reliably distinguish them.

faqs.question: How long does pseudopelade of Brocq last?
faqs.answer: Pseudopelade typically progresses over several years before entering a stable or burnt-out phase. The active inflammatory phase may last 2-15 years. Some patients remain in cycles of mild activity and relative quiescence. With treatment, stabilisation is often achieved within 6-12 months.

faqs.question: Can hair transplant fix pseudopelade?
faqs.answer: Hair transplantation into scarred areas is possible only in carefully selected patients whose disease has been fully stable for at least 2 years with no active inflammation on dermoscopy. Graft survival is lower than in non-scarring conditions due to the fibrotic scalp environment. Success rates of 50-80% have been reported in specialist hands.

faqs.question: Is pseudopelade hereditary?
faqs.answer: It is not directly inherited in a simple pattern. An autoimmune predisposition with genetic susceptibility to inflammatory follicular disorders may be inherited. A family history of autoimmune diseases is more common in patients with primary cicatricial alopecias than in the general population.

faqs.question: What is hydroxychloroquine and why is it used for pseudopelade?
faqs.answer: Hydroxychloroquine is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory properties that reduce lymphocyte activation and inflammatory signalling. It is the most widely used oral agent for primary lymphocytic cicatricial alopecias including pseudopelade of Brocq. It is taken daily and requires annual eye checks to monitor for rare retinal toxicity.

faqs.question: Does pseudopelade affect eyebrows or body hair?
faqs.answer: Pseudopelade of Brocq almost exclusively affects the scalp. Unlike alopecia universalis, it does not typically involve eyebrows, eyelashes, or body hair. Any concurrent hair loss at other body sites should prompt re-evaluation of the diagnosis.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done for pseudopelade?
faqs.answer: Standard investigations include antinuclear antibody (ANA), anti-dsDNA, complement levels, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate to exclude lupus; thyroid function and anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies to screen for thyroid autoimmunity; and G6PD before starting hydroxychloroquine.

faqs.question: When should I see a dermatologist for suspected pseudopelade?
faqs.answer: See a dermatologist promptly if you notice smooth, progressively enlarging bald patches on the scalp — even if painless. Because scarring is permanent, early diagnosis and treatment preserve more follicles than delayed referral. Any bald patch that is not regrowing after 3 months warrants specialist assessment.

faqs.question: What does pseudopelade look like on dermoscopy?
faqs.answer: On dermoscopy, pseudopelade shows loss of follicular ostia within the patch, white fibrous areas at the centre, and subtle perifollicular erythema or fine scale at the very active margin. These features differ from alopecia areata (yellow dots, exclamation-mark hairs) and confirm the scarring nature of the condition.

faqs.question: Can pseudopelade of Brocq be related to Lyme disease?
faqs.answer: Some European studies have found serological evidence of Borrelia burgdorferi in patients with pseudopelade, suggesting a possible infectious trigger in endemic regions. North American studies have not replicated this finding consistently. Testing for Borrelia is recommended in patients from endemic areas, and confirmed Lyme disease is treated separately.

faqs.question: How do I manage the emotional impact of permanent hair loss from pseudopelade?
faqs.answer: Psychological support is an important part of management. Cognitive-behavioural therapy helps with acceptance and coping. Peer support through organisations like the Cicatricial Alopecia Research Foundation connects patients with others in the same situation. Scalp camouflage products and custom wigs can substantially reduce the visible impact.

metaDescription: Pseudopelade of Brocq causes permanent smooth bald patches from scalp scarring. Learn diagnosis, hydroxychloroquine treatment, and prognosis.
